content.problemStatement:
There are only 222 type of denominations in Chefland:


Coins worth 111 rupee each
Notes worth 101010 rupees each
Chef wants to pay his friend exactly XXX rupees.
What is the minimum number of **coins** Chef needs to pay exactly XXX rupees?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing a single integer XXX.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of coins Chef needs to pay exactly XXX rupees.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X, the amount Chef has to pay.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of 1‑rupee coins required to pay exactly X rupees.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each X we use as many 10‑rupee notes as possible. The remaining amount X % 10 is paid with 1‑rupee coins. Hence: 53%10=3, 100%10=0, 9%10=9, 11%10=1.

solution.approach: Only two denominations exist: a 1‑rupee coin and a 10‑rupee note. Since notes do not count towards the answer, we should use as many 10‑rupee notes as possible and pay the remaining amount with 1‑rupee coins. The remainder after dividing X by 10 is exactly the number of coins needed. Hence answer = X % 10 for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
